description: Explains Craft's purpose as a small cross-platform Minecraft-like game, how the main client organizes world state and rendering, and how configuration, persistence, multiplayer, and build setup fit together.
document:
Craft is a small Minecraft-like game for Windows, Mac OS X, and Linux. The README frames it as only a few thousand lines of C using modern OpenGL shaders, with online multiplayer available through a Python-based server. Its feature list shows the intended scope: procedural terrain, several block types, plants and transparent blocks, simple clouds, a day and night cycle, a textured sky dome, persistent world edits, and multiplayer. The project is not presented as an engine toolkit; it is a compact playable client whose source demonstrates the major pieces needed for a voxel world.

The main client is concentrated in `src/main.c`, where the top-level data structures describe the game's runtime model. `Model` is the global application state: it keeps the GLFW window, worker threads, loaded chunks, known players, typing and chat state, framebuffer dimensions, view flags, online or offline mode, database path, server address, day length, and recently selected or copied blocks. This makes the file the orchestration point for input, networking, persistence, chunk management, and rendering. A chunk is the basic world unit, storing block and light maps, signs, its chunk coordinates, dirty status, vertical bounds, face counts, and OpenGL buffers. Player state is separated into position and rotation snapshots so remote players can be interpolated smoothly.

Startup in `main` builds the client environment before the game world is entered. It initializes curl and GLFW, creates a window from the configured size or fullscreen mode, installs input callbacks, creates the OpenGL context, enables culling and depth testing, and loads the block, font, sky, and sign textures. It then loads shader programs for blocks, lines, text, and sky rendering, recording the attribute and uniform locations in a small rendering descriptor. Command line arguments choose whether the client starts online against a host and optional port or offline against the default save database. The configured chunk creation, render, deletion, and sign radii are copied from `src/config.h`, and worker threads are started to prepare chunk data outside the main frame path.

The outer game loop reinitializes the persistence and networking layer whenever the mode changes. Offline play opens the local database, while online play may use a cache database, connects the client socket, sends the protocol version, and performs login. The reset path clears chunks, players, chat, observation state, flight mode, selected item, and the day clock before loading the saved player position. During each frame the client updates framebuffer scale, time and FPS bookkeeping, mouse look, movement, incoming server messages, periodic database commits, and throttled position updates to the server. It then deletes distant chunks, refreshes the local player buffer, interpolates other players, and renders the sky, visible chunks, signs, players, wireframe selection, HUD, chat text, and optional picture-in-picture observation view.

The configuration header gives Craft much of its easy-to-read shape. It contains default window behavior, VSync, database and cache choices, day length, mouse orientation, feature toggles for rendered overlays and world elements, key bindings, chunk radii, chunk size, and database commit interval. `CMakeLists.txt` builds a single `craft` executable from all C files under the source directory plus bundled copies of GLEW, PNG loading, noise generation, SQLite, and tinycthread. GLFW is added from the dependency tree, CURL is required from the system for authentication-related HTTPS support, and platform-specific libraries are linked for Apple, Unix, and MinGW builds. Together, the README, configuration, build file, and main client show a self-contained voxel game whose purpose is to combine procedural chunks, OpenGL rendering, SQLite-backed edits, and optional socket-based multiplayer in a compact C codebase.
